summary: Added tools/split_suggester.py, a 569-line Python utility for analyzing monolithic ASM .s files and suggesting source file boundaries. The tool uses .s text/data/sdata2 parsing, float literal usage, duplicate float constants, suspicious sdata2 padding, intra-file function-call dependencies, and optional map-file names to recommend splits and optionally generate .c template files with function header comments.

key_files.details: Introduced a Python script for suggesting file splits from a source ASM file and optionally creating corresponding .c files with function metadata comments.

what_changed: Added a new command-line tool, split_suggester.py, accepting a required .s file and an optional .map file.
Implemented parsing of ASM comments, labels, .global markers, .section .data, and .section .sdata2 to infer functions, byte totals, float literals, and section boundaries.
Added optional map-file parsing so generated function headers can include known function names, while filtering out names starting with zz or ending with an underscore.
Added Function objects that track a function's label name, optional map name, start address, end address, source lines, referenced float labels, and computed length.

Implemented a first split pass based on float literal usage: when a function uses a new sdata2 label for a float value already used earlier in the current candidate file, the script treats it as evidence of a file boundary.
Implemented detection of suspicious padding between single-precision floats in sdata2 and uses that as another file-break signal.
Implemented a second split pass that checks function call dependencies and moves functions from the previous candidate file into the current one if later functions require them.
Printed summary diagnostics including total function count, total float count, and aligned byte counts for text, data, and sdata2 sections.
Added interactive prompting to create output .c files under a derived src path, including overwrite handling for existing files.

Generated .c templates include an include line for the inferred header, function start/end/length comments, optional map names, a scratch-link placeholder comment, and comments listing float literals referenced by each function.
smart_moves: Used float literal reuse as a practical boundary heuristic, which is specific to how split translation units often duplicate constants in sdata2.

Combined multiple weak signals rather than relying on one heuristic: duplicate float constants, padding between float literals, branch/function-end recognition, .global markers, and function-call dependency correction.
Added a second dependency-aware pass after the initial literal-based split so helper functions needed by later functions are kept with the file that needs them.
Computed and displayed aligned section byte totals, giving decompilers a quick sanity check against expected object sizes.
Made map-file support optional, allowing the tool to work on raw labels while improving generated headers when map information is available.
Generated starter .c files only after an interactive confirmation and included overwrite safeguards, reducing risk from a workflow automation script.
Supported both Python 2 and Python 3 input handling, which likely mattered for the repository/tooling environment at the time.
decomp_lessons: When splitting large ASM files, sdata2 float constants can be strong evidence for translation-unit boundaries because equivalent values may appear under separate labels when they came from separate source files.
Padding in literal pools can be a useful boundary hint, but this PR treats it as suspicious rather than definitive and cross-checks it during analysis.
File-boundary tools should account for call dependencies after initial boundary detection; otherwise, helper functions may be stranded in the wrong generated source file.
Optional map-file integration is useful for workflow tools: raw ASM labels are enough to operate, but map names improve generated comments and human readability.
Generated source templates should preserve evidence useful for matching work, including function address ranges, byte lengths, map names, and referenced constants.
For decomp tooling, interactive file generation should be cautious about existing files and clearly state the output directory.
naming_conventions: Tool file added under tools/ with snake_case name: split_suggester.py.
Generated C files are named from the input ASM basename plus a numeric suffix, such as filename1.c, filename2.c, etc.
Generated C files include a header named after the input basename: #include "<filename>.h".
For fighter character paths ending in asm/ft/chara, the script derives a src subfolder named using an ft-prefixed character folder convention, e.g. 'ft' plus capitalized characters from the filename.
Map names beginning with 'zz' or ending with '_' are intentionally ignored for generated function-name comments.
The script follows internal camelCase variable names such as mapFilePath, currentFunction, reachedDataSection, functionEndLikely, and sdataBytes rather than the repository's common C naming style.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Parses decompiled ASM comment address lines beginning with /* and counts each code line as 4 text bytes.
Recognizes likely function ends from lines ending in blr, while checking later labels and branch targets to avoid treating a branch-to-after-blr case as a true function boundary.
Treats .global lines as evidence that a function boundary is certain.
Extracts function start addresses from the first code line of each Function and sets previous function end addresses from the next function start.
Detects lfs and lfd references to lbl_ labels to infer whether sdata2 constants are single or double precision floats.
Uses struct.unpack with big-endian formats >f and >d to convert .4byte values from sdata2 into comparable float values.
Accounts for .data byte totals from .4byte and aligned .asciz lengths, then aligns text, data, and sdata2 byte totals to 8 bytes.
Uses duplicate float values under different labels as evidence that the current function may belong to a new file.
Checks function-line text for other known function names, excluding label definitions, to infer required functions for the dependency pass.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review records were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR body described the tool as useful for finding file boundaries and splitting .s files, and noted that it can create .c files with function headers after printing recommendations.
follow_up_queries: Find later changes to tools/split_suggester.py to see whether heuristics, Python compatibility, or path handling were adjusted.
Search for PRs that used split_suggester.py output to split specific ASM files.
Look for tooling discussions about sdata2 float literal duplication as a file boundary heuristic.
Search for review or follow-up issues mentioning paddingBreaks, susPadding, or generated function headers.
